text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP VISIT NOTE
Patient: Raymond Kowalski | DOB: [on file] | MRN: [on file]
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Return visit for integration of repeat molecular profiling results and ongoing disease management. Patient presents with his wife.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 59-year-old male with a well-established diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3N2aM1b) moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with liver and lung metastases, KRAS exon 2 codon 12 G12D mutant, pMMR/MSS, BRAF wild-type. He is now approximately 18-19 months from his initial diagnosis and is currently receiving second-line therapy with FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (Cyramza).

Briefly, his oncologic history is as follows: He initially presented with synchronous liver-only disease (four hepatic lesions, bilobar) and was treated with modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab for 12 cycles in conversion/induction intent, achieving a confirmed partial response (CEA nadir 22.8 ng/mL from a baseline of 148.2). He transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab per an OPTIMOX-type strategy. Approximately 10 months into his overall treatment course, surveillance imaging demonstrated disease progression with emergence of three new bilateral pulmonary nodules and hepatic re-growth of the Segment VI and IVb lesions, accompanied by a CEA rise to 67.3 ng/mL. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab at that time, achieving a partial response with hepatic and pulmonary disease regression (CEA declined to 44.1 ng/mL). Most recent imaging (approximately 7 months into second-line therapy, last month) demonstrated borderline stable disease with all lesions showing minimal interval increase not meeting threshold for unequivocal progression, and CEA trending modestly upward to 51.8 ng/mL. CA 19-9 was 162 U/mL.

In order to better characterize the molecular evolution of his disease and evaluate for potential acquired resistance mechanisms, a CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule was performed approximately one month ago. Pathology confirmed metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma consistent with colorectal primary (CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-, TTF-1-). Repeat comprehensive genomic profiling (FoundationOne CDx) has now returned and is reviewed in detail at today's visit.

Key findings on repeat NGS: retained baseline alterations (KRAS G12D AF 47%, TP53 R248W, APC E1554*, SMAD4 R361H, CDKN2A homozygous deletion). New/potentially acquired alterations: PIK3CA E545K (AF 18%), KRAS G12V as a secondary subclonal mutation (AF 11%), MYC amplification (CN 8.2), and RB1 Q494* truncating mutation (AF 14%). MSS phenotype retained. ERBB2 not amplified. No NTRK fusions. TMB 6 muts/Mb.

From a symptom standpoint, Mr. Kowalski reports doing reasonably well. His main complaints are ongoing mild fatigue (he rates it 3-4/10 in severity), which has been manageable and he attributes in part to his cancer but also to somewhat disrupted sleep. He continues to have mild tingling in his fingertips and toes bilaterally -- residual from his prior oxaliplatin exposure -- which has not worsened on FOLFIRI. He reports occasional loose stools (1-2 times per day), controlled with as-needed loperamide. No nausea, vomiting, or significant mucositis. No chest pain, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis. No hematochezia or melena. No focal neurologic symptoms. He denies pain at rest though notes mild right upper quadrant aching after meals, which he describes as a longstanding and low-grade symptom he attributes to his liver lesions.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue as above. Weight stable over last month (+/- 1 lb by patient report). No fever, chills, or night sweats.
Gastrointestinal: Grade 1 intermittent loose stools, loperamide-responsive. No nausea or vomiting at present. Mild postprandial right upper quadrant discomfort as noted. No dysphagia or early satiety.
Respiratory: No cough, dyspnea, or hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: Blood pressure monitoring at home: generally 134-142/82-90 mmHg range on dual antihypertensive regimen. No chest pain or palpitations.
Neurologic: Residual peripheral tingling in bilateral fingertips and toes, unchanged (Grade 1, baseline for this patient post-oxaliplatin). No new neurologic symptoms.
Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain.
Skin/integumentary: No significant hand-foot syndrome flares recently.
Psychosocial: Patient reports moderate emotional strain related to his diagnosis. He and his wife are managing; they have accepted the referrals to social work and palliative care and have their first palliative care appointment scheduled next week.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital signs: BP 138/84 mmHg (right arm, seated), HR 74 bpm (regular), RR 16, SpO2 97% on room air, Temperature 98.2 F, Weight 176 lbs (BMI 27.2).

General: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued affect but conversational and in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Accompanied by wife.
HEENT: Normocephalic. Sclerae anicteric. Oral mucosa moist without ulceration. No temporal wasting.
Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. Effort adequate.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1/S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.
Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant. Liver edge palpable approximately 2-3 cm below the right costal margin; mildly firm, nontender to light touch. No splenomegaly. No ascites appreciated. No palpable sigmoid or left lower quadrant mass. Bowel sounds present and normal in quality.
Port site (right chest wall): Subcutaneous port is intact, no overlying erythema, swelling, or tenderness. Port accessed without difficulty.
Extremities: No cyanosis, clubbing, or significant edema (trace bilateral ankle edema, unchanged). Distal pulses intact.
Neurologic: Alert, cooperative. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Diminished vibration sense in bilateral great toes (chronic, unchanged), intact proprioception. No ataxia on tandem gait. Hand grip strength symmetric and appropriate. Finger-to-nose testing normal.
Skin: Mild diffuse alopecia consistent with ongoing irinotecan exposure. No significant hand-foot changes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC: WBC 4.1 K/uL; Hgb 9.9 g/dL (Grade 1 anemia, mildly worsened from 10.0 last visit); MCV 88 fL; Platelets 162 K/uL; ANC 1.5 K/uL (Grade 1 neutropenia, borderline, no febrile events).
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 102, CO2 26, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60), Glucose 104 mg/dL.
Liver function tests: Total bilirubin 0.9 mg/dL; ALT 74 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 62 last visit); AST 69 U/L; Alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L (mildly elevated). Albumin 3.6 g/dL (mildly decreased).
Tumormarkers: CEA 51.8 ng/mL (previous 44.1 ng/mL, mild uptrend); CA 19-9 162 U/mL (previous 134 U/mL).
Urine protein:creatinine ratio: 0.24 (monitoring for ramucirumab-related proteinuria, remains below threshold for dose modification).
Coagulation: INR 1.1, PTT 31 sec.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (reviewed, approximately 1 month prior): As discussed above -- borderline stable disease. Sigmoid primary minimally increased (2.8 x 2.5 cm). Hepatic lesions with minimal but not unequivocal growth (Segment VI 1.9 x 1.7 cm, IVb 0.9 x 0.8 cm, Segment II 0.6 x 0.5 cm). Pulmonary nodules stable to minimally changed (right lower lobe 0.8 x 0.7 cm, left upper lobe 0.6 x 0.5 cm, right middle lobe 0.4 x 0.4 cm). No new metastatic sites. The overall impression was stable disease though with an early directional trend toward slow progression.

Repeat biopsy specimen: Right lower lobe pulmonary nodule core needle biopsy confirming metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IV (M1b) SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA, KRAS G12D MUTANT, pMMR/MSS -- CURRENTLY BORDERLINE STABLE DISEASE ON SECOND-LINE FOLFIRI/RAMUCIRUMAB

Clinical status: Mr. Kowalski remains with an ECOG performance status of 1, functional and living at home with his wife, although his disease has shown a directional trend toward losing control on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab over the past several months following an initial partial response. His tumor markers are slowly climbing, his LFTs are creeping upward (likely reflecting modest hepatic progression), and the modest size increases on imaging, while not formally meeting any specific progression threshold, paint a picture of slowly evolving disease. It would be reasonable to image again in approximately 4-6 weeks to further characterize the trajectory.

Molecular evolution and implications: Today's NGS results are reviewed in detail with Mr. Kowalski and his wife, in language they can understand. The key takeaway is that his cancer has undergone molecular evolution while on chemotherapy -- this is expected behavior in metastatic colorectal cancer and reflects selective pressure from his prior FOLFOX and current FOLFIRI exposure. Several newly detected alterations are noted:

   - PIK3CA E545K (PI3K pathway activation): This is an acquired or newly detectable mutation seen in approximately 10-15% of mCRC and can be a mechanism of resistance to VEGF-pathway inhibitors and chemotherapy in some settings. There are no immediately actionable FDA-approved therapies targeting PIK3CA E545K in colorectal cancer at this time, though PI3K inhibitors and combinations remain under investigation. Clinical trial participation may be relevant here.
   - KRAS G12V subclone (AF 11%): The emergence of a secondary KRAS-mutant subclone alongside the dominant KRAS G12D suggests intratumoral heterogeneity and clonal evolution. This further confirms that anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) remains contraindicated -- this patient has both a dominant and a subclonal RAS-mutant population.
   - MYC amplification (CN 8.2): High-level MYC amplification is associated with aggressive disease biology and may contribute to chemotherapy resistance. No directly targetable therapy available.
   - RB1 truncating mutation (AF 14%): Emerging RB1 loss has implications for cell cycle regulation and may confer cross-resistance to certain cell cycle-directed agents, though not directly actionable in this context.
   - MSS phenotype retained: Confirmed MSS on repeat biopsy. This means checkpoint inhibitor therapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) remains ineffective and is not an option. There are emerging data on combining checkpoint inhibition with other agents in MSS mCRC (e.g., regorafenib combinations, MEK inhibitors), but none are yet standard of care.
   - ERBB2 not amplified, NTRK fusions absent: No actionable target in these pathways.

Patient was counseled thoroughly that no clearly actionable targetable alteration has emerged, and that his molecular profile does not open new targeted therapy options at this time outside of standard third-line chemotherapy or a clinical trial. He expressed understanding and continued interest in pursuing a clinical trial at the time of next progression. I have agreed to prospectively query available institutional and national trials for molecular eligibility (particularly PI3K pathway trials, KRAS G12D-directed therapy trials -- sotorasib and adagrasib were not developed for colorectal as primary approval but there are combination trials underway). Will refer to our clinical trials office for a dedicated consultation.

Plan for current regimen: Given borderline stable disease -- not clearly progressive, not clearly responding -- we will continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab and repeat CT imaging in 5-6 weeks to clarify the trajectory. If repeat imaging confirms unequivocal progression, we will transition to third-line therapy.

2. TREATMENT-RELATED TOXICITIES

Hypertension: BP 138/84 mmHg today. Grade 1, well-managed on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 5 mg daily. Continue current regimen with home blood pressure monitoring. Target <140/90 mmHg.

Anemia: Hgb 9.9 g/dL, mild decline from last visit. Patient reports no dyspnea with ordinary activity. Will recheck with next cycle. Iron studies and B12/folate levels ordered today (mCRC patients on chronic 5-FU can develop folate depletion and iron-deficiency anemia). No transfusion threshold met at this time. If Hgb drops below 8.0 or patient becomes symptomatic, will consider transfusion vs. erythropoiesis-stimulating agent discussion.

Neutropenia: ANC 1.5 K/uL, borderline Grade 1. No febrile neutropenia events to date. Next cycle will proceed with CBC nadir check. No G-CSF prophylaxis indicated currently.

Diarrhea: Grade 1, intermittent, loperamide-responsive. Patient reminded of aggressive loperamide dosing protocol. Dietary guidance reviewed.

Residual oxaliplatin neuropathy: Grade 1, stable, not worsening on irinotecan-based therapy as expected. Continue monitoring.

Urine protein: UPC ratio 0.24, below threshold for ramucirumab dose modification (hold threshold: UPC >=2.0 or urine protein >=3g/24h). Continue monitoring each cycle.

Liver function: Mild transaminase elevation (ALT 74, AST 69, ALP 118) likely reflecting hepatic metastatic burden. Albumin mildly decreased at 3.6. Will monitor trajectory; if albumin continues to decline or bilirubin rises, this may affect future chemotherapy candidacy and warrants reconsideration of treatment approach.

3. GOALS OF CARE AND PSYCHOSOCIAL

Goals of care discussion reaffirmed today in the presence of Mr. Kowalski and his wife. Patient has a clear and realistic understanding that his disease is not curable with current therapies. His primary goals remain to prolong his life while maintaining quality of life and the ability to remain at home, engage with family, and continue light activities. He has completed his advance directive and designated his wife as healthcare proxy.

Palliative care consultation: First appointment scheduled next week. Encouraged to discuss symptom management, goals of care, and emotional support needs at that visit.

Social work: Referral accepted and confirmed. Support resources discussed including financial counseling given ongoing treatment costs.

Clinical trials: Patient remains interested and will be referred to the clinical trials office this week.

4. FOLLOW-UP

Return to clinic in 2 weeks for Cycle [next] of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab with interim labs.
Repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 5-6 weeks.
Clinical trials consultation arranged.
Iron studies, B12, folate results to be reviewed at next visit.
Palliative care appointment next week.

Time spent in counseling and care coordination: approximately 55 minutes.

[Electronically signed -- Attending Oncologist, MD]
